claimed_contribution: Introduces psychological distance as a situational factor that changes how consumers process multidigit numbers containing separators.
Shows that construal-level patterns from verbal and visual domains do not directly generalize to numerical comparison, because distance shifts the relative weight of left versus right digits.
Provides evidence that perceived digit diagnosticity underlies the effect and identifies boundary conditions based on rating-stability beliefs and separator presence.
context: The paper studies consumer numerical judgments in marketplace-like settings where values contain commas or decimal points (e.g., ratings, prices, mileage, salary, index forecasts). Empirical tasks include restaurant voucher choice, hotel willingness-to-pay, and used-car choice scenarios.
design_and_data: Four experiments test the theory using psychological-distance manipulations and separator-based numbers. Study 1 used a 2 (distance: near vs far) x 2 (digit position: left vs right) x 4 (distance-dimension replicates: temporal, spatial, social, probabilistic) mixed design with MTurk participants (n=210) and measured perceived diagnosticity of left versus right digits. Study 2 (Credamo, China; n=200) used a temporally near vs far incentive-compatible restaurant-voucher choice task, measured digit diagnosticity and task involvement, and tested mediation. Study 3 (MTurk; n=210) manipulated spatial distance in a hotel WTP task and measured belief in rating stability as a moderator, and Study 4 (MTurk; n=400) used a 2 (social distance) x 2 (comma present vs absent) design in a used-car choice scenario.
main_findings: Greater psychological distance decreased the perceived diagnosticity of digits to the right of a separator more than digits to the left.
Temporal distance changed restaurant choice patterns in line with reduced reliance on right digits, and this effect was mediated by relative left-versus-right digit diagnosticity.
The effect was attenuated when participants believed numerical ratings were stable over time and when the separator cue was removed.
one_line_summary: Across four experiments, the paper finds that increasing psychological distance makes people downweight digits to the right of a comma or decimal point, altering perceived numerical differences and related choices.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior numerical cognition work by adding psychological distance as a situational driver of how different digit positions are used in separator-based numbers. It also shows that standard construal-level expectations about distance and perceived similarity do not straightforwardly carry over to numerical comparison, and it positions perceived digit diagnosticity as a mechanism.
research_gap: Prior research on numerical cognition largely emphasized framing and presentation characteristics of numbers, leaving unclear whether psychological distance as a situational factor changes how people compare separator-based numbers. The paper also identifies that existing construal-level findings from verbal and visual comparisons may not directly apply to digit-by-digit numerical processing.
research_question: How does psychological distance affect comparative judgments of numbers that contain a separator, such as commas or decimal points? Specifically, does increasing distance reduce the perceived diagnosticity and decision weight of right-side digits more than left-side digits, and when is this effect attenuated?
